name: Brine Pickles
subtitle: Any vegetable, saltwater, time - the universal lacto-ferment
blrNote: KR Market is your playground - cucumbers, carrots, beans, cauliflower, radish, all dirt cheap and year-round. At BLR's 25°C, expect half-sours in 3 days, full sours by day 7. Use rock salt from any kirana store.

ingredients.name: Mixed vegetables
substitutions: Any firm vegetable - cucumber, carrot, beans, cauliflower, radish, turnip

IN.where: KR Market, any sabziwala - ₹20–60/kg depending on vegetable
Any kirana - mustard seeds are a South Indian pantry staple

equipment.name: Weight to keep vegetables submerged
equipment.notes: Small zip-lock bag filled with brine, or a clean stone

tldr: Dissolve salt in water. Submerge any vegetables. Wait. The simplest fermentation technique in existence.

steps.title: Make the brine
steps.instruction: Dissolve 30g non-iodised salt in 1 litre of unchlorinated water. Stir until fully dissolved. This gives you a 3% brine - the sweet spot for most vegetable ferments.
tips: 3% is mild and crisp. Go to 5% for longer storage or softer vegetables like cucumbers.
Taste the brine - it should taste like pleasant seawater, not aggressively salty
Always weigh your salt with a kitchen scale - a tablespoon of fine salt and coarse salt differ by almost 50%

steps.title: Prepare vegetables
steps.instruction: Wash vegetables. Cut into uniform pieces - spears, coins, or florets. Keep sizes consistent so they ferment evenly. Add optional garlic, mustard seeds, or other aromatics to the jar first.
tips: Cucumbers: cut off blossom end (contains enzymes that soften pickles)
Carrots and beans: leave whole or halve lengthwise
steps.checkpoint: Uniform vegetable pieces ready to pack

steps.title: Pack and submerge
steps.instruction: Pack vegetables tightly into the jar. Pour brine over until vegetables are fully submerged with 2–3cm of brine above them. Place a weight on top to keep everything under the brine.
tips: Tight packing prevents vegetables from floating above the brine
Leave 3–4cm headspace above the brine for bubbling
Place the jar on a plate - active fermentation often pushes brine over the rim in the first 48 hours
steps.checkpoint: All vegetables submerged under brine with weight in place

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover loosely (cloth or loose lid - gas needs to escape). Leave at room temperature away from direct sun. In BLR, taste daily from day 3. Half-sour by day 3–4, full sour by day 7–10.
tips: Bubbles forming within 24–48 hours means fermentation is active
Brine will turn cloudy - this is normal and good
Skim any white film (kahm yeast) from the surface - harmless but tastes yeasty
steps.checkpoint: Vegetables taste pleasantly sour and tangy

steps.title: Refrigerate
steps.instruction: When vegetables reach your preferred sourness, seal the jar and refrigerate. Cold slows fermentation nearly to a halt.
tips: The brine is drinkable and deeply probiotic - don't discard it
Re-use the leftover brine as a starter for your next batch - it jump-starts fermentation and cuts a day off the timeline
steps.checkpoint: Tangy, crunchy, sour pickles in cold storage

images.caption: Brine Pickles
images.attribution: Pickled cucumber in glass jar by Mariuszjbie
images.alt: Brine Pickles - Any vegetable, saltwater, time - the universal lacto-ferment

culturalContext.story: Brine pickling is arguably the oldest food preservation technique after drying. Before refrigeration, before canning, before any technology at all - humans discovered that saltwater keeps food from spoiling. Every civilisation with access to salt independently developed some version of brine fermentation.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Archaeological evidence of brine-pickled cucumbers dates to 2030 BCE in Mesopotamia. The word "pickle" derives from the Dutch "pekel" (brine). Cleopatra attributed her beauty to pickles. Roman soldiers carried them on campaigns.
culturalContext.significance: Brine pickles are the template for all lacto-fermentation. If you understand this technique - salt + water + submersion + time - you understand the principle behind sauerkraut, kimchi, and most vegetable ferments. This is fermentation at its most elemental.
culturalContext.funFact: New York City's Lower East Side was once the pickle capital of America. In the early 1900s, pickle vendors sold brine-fermented cucumbers from barrels on the street. The famous "pickle guys" of Essex Street still operate today.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Vegetables floating above brine
thingsToAccountFor.description: Anything above the brine will mould. Always use a weight. A zip-lock bag filled with brine works perfectly - if it leaks, it's just more brine.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Soft or mushy pickles
thingsToAccountFor.description: Over-fermentation or too-warm conditions can soften vegetables. In BLR summers, check earlier and refrigerate sooner. Adding grape leaves, oak leaves, or a pinch of calcium chloride helps maintain crunch.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Salt concentration matters
thingsToAccountFor.description: 3% is standard. Below 2%, harmful bacteria can outcompete lactobacillus. Above 5%, fermentation slows significantly. Measure by weight, not volume.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Drain vegetables, slice thinly, dehydrate at 40°C for 8–12 hours.
dehydratorIntegration.result: Sour vegetable chips - tangy, crunchy, deeply flavoured snack.
tips: Carrot and beet pickles dehydrate especially well
Cucumber pickles become chewy rather than crunchy - still delicious

variations.name: Deli-style dill pickles
variations.description: Cucumbers in 5% brine with dill, garlic, mustard seeds, and peppercorns. The classic American deli pickle.

variations.name: Indian-spiced brine pickles
variations.description: Add mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chillies, and turmeric to the brine. Familiar spice profile, unfamiliar technique.

variations.name: Quick pickled beans
variations.description: French beans in 3% brine with garlic. Ready in 3–4 days. Snappy, sour, addictive.
